title: Exposure To A Bloodborne Pathogen Is Defined In Part As
source_filename: Exposure To A Bloodborne Pathogen Is Defined In Part As.docx

qas.q: All body fluids except sweat should be considered potentially infectious
Needlestick injuries are very common
Transmission of a bloodborne pathogen after an occupational exposure is very common
Exposure to a bloodborne pathogen is defined in part as?
qas.a: Contact of a mucous membrane/non-intact skin with a potentially infectious body fluid or secretion

qas.q: The risk of developing an HBV infection after an occupational exposure
The risk of developing an HCV infection after an occupational exposure
The risk of developing an HIV infection after an occupational exposure
The risk of transmission of a bloodborne pathogen depends in part on
qas.a: The viral load of the patient and the type of injury/exposure

qas.q: Employers must offer HBV vaccination to their employees
Standard precautions include
qas.a: Hand hygiene, PPE safe injection practices, and respiratory hygiene/cough etiqutte

qas.q: Alcohol based hand sanitizers are less effective than soap and water
soap and water should be used for handwashing
qas.a: when the hands are visibly soiled

qas.q: Hand hygiene should be done
qas.a: before and after providing patient care

qas.q: The choice of which PPE to use is based on
qas.a: The risk of exposure the specific care situation

qas.q: PPE should be donned and removed in a specific sequence
Handwashing is the most effective way of preventing the transmission of pathogens
If an employee wears gloves handwashing is not necessary
IV catheters needles and syringes should never be re used for another patient
Contaminated needles and sharps should not be re capped
The def of an exposure to a bloodborne pathogen is in part of
qas.a: Contact of the eyes mouth or non intact skin with blood

qas.q: An exposure to a bloodborne pathogen can cause significant emotional harm
Employees should report an exposure to a bloodborne pathogen
Immediately after an eye/mucous membrane exposure to blood or potentially infectious body fluids
immediately after a needle stick or a sharps injury
qas.a: wash the wound with soap and water

qas.q: If an employee has received HBV vaccine the patient has an HBV infection and the employee suffers a needlestick
qas.a: The employee does not need to be treated

qas.q: Prophylatic treatment for an HBV exposure can include
There is no effective treatment for HCV exposure
6 weeks after exposure to HCV and the employee has measureable HCV RNA
qas.a: the employee should be referred to a clincican

qas.q: After an exposure to HIV the employee should have
qas.a: HIV antigen antibody test and HBV testing if needed

qas.q: PEP for an HIV exposure
qas.a: Should be started within 2 hours of the exposure

qas.q: A consultation is not needed if PEP will be given 72 hours after an exposure
If the patients rapid HIV test is neg PEP can be stopped
If the patients rapid HIV test is positive PEP should be started
PEP should be given for an HIV exposure
qas.a: If the patients HIV status cannot be determined

qas.q: PEP should not be given 72 hours after an exposure to HIV
A preg employee who has been exposed to HIV
qas.a: should be evaluated and treated using the standard PEP protocol

qas.q: Antiretroviral therapy during pregnancy
Antiretroviral therapy for non-preg patients is
Breastfeeding is contraindicted during HIV PEP
In most cases employees should have HIV testing
qas.a: 6 weeks to 3 months after the exposure
